sentences.text: Especially with the-- with my Polish friends, I was skating with them.
What's-- I mentioned before, I would-- I hate to repeat myself.
It's the only thing where the-- I associated-- the-- they liked me, because they thought-- I don't know.
I think so.
They thought-- I act like they-- I'm-- the-- Polish people was different to say, the Jewish people-- different dress, different-- with always some of them with accent-- not all of them-- like myself.
[LAUGHS] This is the reason they associate with me.
They-- and-- and I enjoyed them, because they been-- good-- very good athletes.
And I consider myself a good athlete in-- in the-- even now, I think-- I believe longevity if I'm-- already now 80-- 82 years.
Because I've been, all my life a good athlete-- a good soccer player, a good bicycle, a good-- especially ice skating.
I loved ice skating until today.
And I even think someday I feel another-- because now I'm after serious surgery, I will feel better.
I go back skating.
I got fine skates.
I paid a few hundred dollar.
I couldn't afford it as something-- a kid in Poland.
I couldn't afford it, something like this.
Not the reason, because I tried to keep myself at a distance from them.
I have give them a chance to see me different, because I had been a good athlete, and I'd been-- I-- we associated with the-- the Polish girls.
We used to go on-- even I belonged to entire-- when I come home from there, I-- I-- I like I-- I-- I got two personalities in myself-- one personality way to Zionism, to this movement.
I feel myself, like, very close.
I-- most education, I took more-- a lot of reading in groups where there-- the more advanced in education.
We advanced ourself.
Or when I went-- I love because of athletic stature.
I-- well, [LAUGHS] it's hard to say.
I say, I've been good.
I've been good.
This, is the-- I think it's a big reason.
And I went with them, I used to go bicycle, go out, and go places.
[LAUGHS] But-- if-- my mother used to say, don't eat [INAUDIBLE].
Don't-- you know, in the home, we got a kosher kitchen, or a kosher-- and she know.
And I didn't give the-- my Polish friends, I didn't give them a chance.
I'm different from them.
I-- I I don't know.
I-- this was my nature.
I-- I've been like this.
And I talked with them.
I've been very serious with them.
I-- I-- I don't try to-- myself, I don't try to-- to be I'm difference.
I-- I-- I'm a sincere person.
Even they share with me-- they got their own-- went to school, to-- to school.
The-- even-- used to-- we had done some special-- a friend of my brother, his girlfriend, I asked him-- I felt I need more education.
I haven't got enough.
It's-- I-- used even the Polish friends to-- to help me with Polish history, like things like that.
I didn't give them a chance to think I'm different.
This is the reason.
[LAUGHS]
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They treat me like-- like I-- I am their owns.
When I went to-- in the night-- special.
I went back to-- to-- to the organization, to-- to-- to my group of people.
When I-- I'm talking when I was a youngster.
It was entire difference.
I give lot of thought, but it's even now, so many years away, I give lot of thought.
Because I-- that's what I've done.
I've been sincere.
I think that's why they accept me.
And I learned from this.
I had-- partners here in United States.
I got to bring this up.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's the same way I learned from this time.
He-- he-- he used to tell me the same thing.
Henry, you are different person than like I dealt with other.
I didn't like it.
He know I didn't like this saying, like I've been told this in Poland.
He was a Canadian.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I live with him.
I done some work with him in-- in a town here in-- in the United States, in Santa Maria.
They build the Vandenberg Airbase.
We done some work with him.
I got a chance to live with him.
He-- all together, until we build a office over there.
I would like to drive every day-- every day 400 miles.
Is-- we-- I live with him.
He told me the same thing.
And I learned it with my Polish friends over there.
I considered them friends.
They were doing everything during the war for Val.
I got in contact with the-- they brought me some food.
And even after the war, I got in touch-- after the war.
We left right away.
He-- he used-- he must be mine age, this-- must be around 35, 30.
He worked in a-- in a big firm, a Polish firm.
He brought me a lot of food, even after the war.
I didn't ask him.
I say, it's all right.
That's what I learned during--
And my-- my sister, this-- she used to say you, they didn't like it, what I was doing.
I took the--
Because it was-- the hate was so big.
She-- when-- sister used to say, [NON-ENGLISH].
You're going to die between gentile-- between Polish people.
Because so been-- big difference, in general.
It was a small percentage like me, very small, but know how to handle.
You've got to have a certain type of intelligence-- how to balance-- with--
Yes.
Yes-- helped me survive things like this.
You was able to go along with people.
You-- you-- you-- later on, during the war, I tried to-- to survive.
You've been separated, like a ghetto.
How to survive?
You have to be in contact with outside people to bring some food-- doing the exchange during this time.
You-- you had to go like this to be organized-- how to survive.
That's what I learned.
Well, I couldn't say I was on by myself-- would lift-- this guy eventually was-- Radom was a city with 35,000 Jews.
They had-- they had to make a living with-- with-- the Polish population, like you run a business.
You run a-- the hate was surely-- the-- the-- is difference.
Here you got the hate, let's say, on each other.
Over there-- the government was like this.
Like, the government, they made you make a sign.
This is to recognize this is a Jewish institution, a Jewish store, a Jewish manufacturer.
All my life.
All my life it was doing, even with Jewish heroes in-- in-- in the-- in the Polish-- I don't know if I find words at this moment.
I know the independence in Poland participate.
Before the war.
Well, we know the-- the stories-- what's-- what's going on before the war.
He say a-- a big percentage for the Jewish population, like I said before, went to other countries, like special of the anti-Semitism education for the children.
They went to Germany before '33.
And Hitler come in '33 to power.
They took the all of them.
They called them [NON-ENGLISH] and sent back to Poland-- took them-- and send them back to Poland.
From them-- from them, we learn what is going on.
They still-- [LAUGHS] --haven't been happy with the Polish.
They've been ridiculous.
Some of them even-- German Jews say to me, if-- [NON-ENGLISH], German that we could die from the Poles.
The Poles was-- the first thing that they done to-- to-- to look us-- for Jews, they get a five pound sugar, or five pound salt, to show this is a [NON-ENGLISH].
Some Jews took off.
And they went outside to try to organize some food or get in contact with some--
This is the start of the war.
This was the start when the Pole-- when the German come.
Maybe three months, four months later, they-- after a few months later, they put them in ghettos.
Yeah.
Well, like, I try to remember.
You say, if you learn when the [NON-ENGLISH] where the-- Hitler send them back to Poland.
What is going on?
We tried to run away.
I took the bicycle.
I ran away to the thousands-- hundreds.
I ran away to the Russian-- to the Russian.
This-- when the German came to Poland.
Before that even, in the 1939.
[NON-ENGLISH] This must be the 9th or 10th month, I believe.
No, before.
[NON-ENGLISH] must be the October-- September-- October-- I don't remember.
I have to figure out.
Yeah, they ran away.
We was trying to cross the Russian zone, not to be with Germans.
My whole family-- my sister-- mine-- my brother's wife went to-- to the-- border from the Russian-Polish border was the--
What happened?
I don't know what happened.
To me?
I went back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I-- sometime, maybe I could say an impulse.
I got my parents.
I got my friends.
I got a lot of-- I left everything-- business.
I accumulated lots of a lot of china and things like this.
I say well, over there you don't know what is there.
Over there is still-- you haven't saw the horrible things-- what they're doing.
And I went back to Radom.
Oh, yes.
I was already--
I-- I was already in the china business, yeah.
I had--
Hm?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I was in china business.
I start with the parents.
He start when he was 12.
You had to-- 14, 15-- you have to work with the parents.
Everybody--
In this time--
Yeah, yeah.
I would say about three, four weeks away.
It's-- I come back, I still tried--
During this--

Three, four weeks, I went-- mine-- brother-- where you say in the Polish army-- he was in the Polish army-- and his wife and child-- I-- when I went away the bicycle, I took my brother's wife and his child.
She's alive.

In the-- the-- her parents was living on the border within Russia-- Wlodawa-- inside been over there after-- been very wealthy, the rich Jews-- very wealthy, got lot of-- lumber mills, in-- storage and-- very rich.
I stayed with them, yeah.
No.
I haven't crossed the-- the border.
I haven't-- I done a lot of thinking.
I say, no.
I'm not-- whatever is going to happen, I got to save whatever.
You left already on the-- a lot of-- accumulated a lot of things.
What you say here, you-- nothing.
I don't know what is going to happen there.
I never saw the Germans yet.
As I-- during the-- the whole time I went in Germany, by the thousands-- 1,000 Germans.
And I was not afraid.
I was young, and I looked-- I was blond.
[LAUGHS] And I looked like Gentile, and they wouldn't--
Hmm?
When I start to see the Germans when I start back home from Wlodawa from-- from the border.
No, no problems.
They wouldn't recognize me.
This is-- I am difference in a-- if they would recognize me I'm Jewish, I'd have to wear the band.
So as soon as they came, every Jew have to show he's Jewish.
Was the same thing I left.
I left-- I got a few places of business.
I got my own place of business is the before I left.
That's what make me run away.
They bombed the-- the city.
I had a business near-- near a factory for ammunition.
And the Germans bombed this place.
And lot of people were killed, and this, that.
But still, I came back.
I tried to salvage lot of things what I left.
During the war is a shortage, everything was-- became so expensive so-- and you tried to-- to make a living in this help.
Yeah.
They still been living in the same place, in the weeks, or maybe 1, 2, 3, or 4-- I don't remember exactly.
I cannot give you a date.
They start to concentrate-- to organize ghettos, and that's it.
I [LAUGHS] I went the same way where all of them Jews went-- to ghettos.
In Radom.
The-- let's say you've got-- like I said before-- like 35,000 Jews.
They give them-- let be 1/10 of the city, a part where the Jewish people used to live-- concentrate.
With-- with everybody-- with my parents, with my friends, with-- with-- with-- with the leadership.
Like the hell-- where you got to learn to--
You learn to live with this.

Well, let's say if-- if my parents lived in-- let's say in a house, like a-- well, let's say, like, here, I would say a three bedroom house-- you-- you went-- they-- if they make a place like this, you-- you have to take it in people.
Let's-- the 35,000 Jews lived-- let's say they give you a place where 1,000 Jews lived-- 10 in a-- in a-- in a room, or whatever.
It's a lot to make-- it's going to be a big story.
You see, it was not so easy, it-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] People, they had money.
I actually, like I said-- I came back.
And I tried to salvage.
I accumulated.
You had some money in comparison with people what they haven't got money.
It's-- for money, you could try to, say, to-- to-- get a better place to live.
I had a place.
We used to have-- let's say, a-- a-- job in place for in china, a job.
We've been jobless.
It's a wholesale.
I had a-- a office over there.
And I moved in the office.
You pay money.
The administration-- you know, the Germans, they haven't done the-- the dirty thing.
The dirty thing, the Poles had done.
It's for money, you could stay another month, another two months.
You find a time like this.
You-- you find a--
Yeah.
Let's say I got in touch with this department.
There was Poles-- included the head in the department was a German.
The administration was Poland-- and for money.
You give him money, and-- and you-- you was able to stay longer, get a better place to live, until it was harder, harder, until--
I lived in my own little office.
I lived, yeah.
Who will remember?
You bribe not just with money.
You give him a set of china.
You got-- money was-- became right-- lots of-- [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, the Polish zloty was not-- start became less, less [INAUDIBLE] Was less, less-- is-- there was many different ways to do it.
And you learn--
You learn, you learn how to do it.
Yeah.
The rest-- they went-- they went to a smaller place, let's say-- where the ghetto was.
They was in a smaller place, let's say-- all of them in one room start-- before they got an apartment, in their own house.
They moved in a small-- because--
Oh, they stayed in their own building, but they moved to a smaller quarter.
There was-- not their own building.
The-- the Judenrat organized between self.
And they-- and they-- tried to-- to-- to-- to assign many different places according how many people were in most--
--in a time like this, is bribes.
Even the Jews between self, they bribe.
Because they want to live better.
When want-- they got more money.
They can buy more and whatever.
Yeah.
I know the name.
And I-- and I had been very close.
They organized [NON-ENGLISH]-- a police force of Yiddish, because-- if I know, I know.
Yeah.
Some of them was very good people.
They've been-- they've been-- the time made you corrupted.
The time made you became evil-- even like Jewish.
No, no-- positively not-- positively not.
My brother was for a little later one.
They-- they changed the-- the-- you know, if you work with it, you have got to-- you had to be evil.
You've got to-- if not-- they killed themselves.
They was afraid.
No, thank God.
I haven't.
Until every time-- let's say there was 30,000 Jews.
There was 30.
They-- made a smaller ghetto for 18-- let's say I give you approximately, 18,000 Jews.
And later-- later until it comes to a point was from the 30,000 was left 2,000.
Oh, they were sent to death camps.
They went Treblinka--
The-- most of the people didn't believe this is possible they're going to do with them what they're doing.
They--
At first, I-- we didn't believe it's going to be so bad-- they going to kill you.
They're going to-- little by little, you start to believe this-- everything.

Because in these camps, it was brave kids that ran away that-- that told the people, take a look, whatever-- to organize a [NON-ENGLISH]-- to try to organize themself or to make a revolution, or-- it was impossible to do.
And like the-- they took away the-- the-- they new the leadership.
They took-- before they took the-- the-- the intelligentsia, let's say the profession, they took away the doctors.
They done this-- this way--
